name: the-truth-about-financial-freedom
description:
David Chilton's The Wealthy Barber Returns (also known as The Truth About Financial Freedom) — an executable toolkit that applies common-sense principles to saving, spending, borrowing, and investing. Cuts through the complexity of personal finance with psychological insight, behavioral economics, and practical habits anyone can follow.

Covers 5 use cases:
① Saving & Spending — build a sustainable savings habit without deprivation, understand the psychological traps that lead to overspending
② Debt Management — distinguish good debt from bad debt, develop a strategy to reduce and eliminate consumer debt
③ Investing Fundamentals — avoid common investing mistakes, understand why index funds beat active management, ignore the noise
④ Behavioral Finance — recognize the psychological biases that sabotage financial decisions, from the endowment effect to loss aversion
⑤ Home & Life Planning — navigate the biggest financial decisions: buying vs renting, mortgages, emergency funds, and estate planning.

Trigger when users say: "I can't save money" "I need financial advice" "How to get out of debt" "Where should I invest" "I spend too much" "Living paycheck to paycheck" "How to build wealth" "Should I buy a house" "Credit card debt" "I need a financial plan" "How to budget" "Stop overspending" "Financial freedom" "Money management" "Personal finance tips"
or mention: Dave Chilton / Wealthy Barber / truth about financial freedom / personal finance / behavioral economics / saving vs spending / index funds / consumer debt / living within means / pay yourself first / emergency fund / mortgage / line of credit / financial independence.

Related skills: rich-dad-poor-dad, i-will-teach-you-to-be-rich, the-millionaire-fastlane, atomic-habits, the-slight-edge.
useCase: Helps anyone take control of their personal finances through common-sense principles — from building saving habits and eliminating debt to investing wisely and making sound life decisions with money.
knownRisks: The book presents a Canadian-centric perspective on financial products (RRSPs, TFSAs, etc.) — users in other countries should adapt to local equivalents.
